agent_reply: Short version: **not in the currently installed workspace yet**.
That is Arduino ESP32 `2.0.17`, still on ESP-IDF 4.4-era APIs. I searched the installed framework and did **not** find `i2s_channel_register_event_callback`.

Important strategy note: even once the new API is available, `i2s_channel_register_event_callback` callbacks run in **ISR context** per Espressifâ€™s docs. So AudioManager should not call `pump_bt2s()` / `pump_mic2bt()` directly from that callback if those functions use mutexes, FIFO operations, `i2s_channel_read/write`, logging, or anything that can block. The callback should only notify a task, for example via `vTaskNotifyGiveFromISR()` or `xQueueSendFromISR()`, and the pump task should do the real work.
I2S ISR callback -> notify audio pump task -> pump_bt2s/pump_mic2bt in task context

Once PlatformIO actually installs the newer framework, yes, we can likely migrate from legacy `driver/i2s.h` + event queue to the new channel API, but the callback should be used as a wake signal only.

user_prompt: what's the best way of telling platformio.ini that we have less RAM available?
agent_reply: `327680` is the normal PlatformIO internal-RAM number for plain ESP32 boards without PSRAM.
`board_upload.maximum_ram_size` only changes PlatformIOâ€™s size accounting/reporting and failure threshold. It does not change the linker script, heap layout, PSRAM enablement, or runtime behavior.
For a cleaner long-term setup, Iâ€™d probably make a custom board JSON, e.g.:
Thatâ€™s the least surprising approach because â€œthis board config has no PSRAMâ€ is captured in one place instead of being split across `build_unflags` and `board_upload.maximum_ram_size`.

One caveat: this does not help IRAM directly. IRAM overflow is governed by the linker script and actual `.iram0.text` placement. The RAM size estimate is just PlatformIOâ€™s static RAM budget check for `.data/.bss`.

user_prompt: If the application uses PSRAM and is based on ESP32 rev. 3 (ECO3), setting CONFIG_ESP32_REV_MIN to 3 disables PSRAM bug workarounds, saving 10 KB or more of IRAM.
what do you think? can I do it? if we buy back enough IRAM we can start using PSRAM again
agent_reply: Your chip reports `Revision: 3.01`, so it is ESP32 ECO3-or-newer. The setting is meant exactly for this case: if you guarantee the firmware only runs on ESP32 rev 3+ silicon, ESP-IDF can drop the old PSRAM cache bug workarounds and save a meaningful chunk of IRAM.
- `Embedded PSRAM: No` is not the blocker. That only means PSRAM is not inside the ESP32 package. Core2-style boards can still have external SPI PSRAM.
- This is only safe if every device you ship/use is rev 3+. If an older ESP32 rev 0/1/2 board gets this firmware, expect boot refusal or bad behavior.
So you are currently paying for the old silicon workaround. Setting min rev to 3 should let IDF drop a bunch of the `CONFIG_SPIRAM_CACHE_LIB*_IN_IRAM` baggage.

My take: this is one of the better IRAM buybacks available to you. Iâ€™d try `CONFIG_ESP32_REV_MIN_3=y` / `CONFIG_ESP32_REV_MIN=3` before the more dangerous ISR-related FreeRTOS/ringbuf flash-placement options. It matches your actual hardware evidence and is less spooky than moving ISR-adjacent functions out of IRAM.

The crash was happening before app startup, inside ESP-IDF flash initialization. The decoded trace pointed at `esp_flash_read_chip_id()` calling `memcpy` while flash cache was disabled. The problem was that `memcpy` had been linked into flash at `0x401f...`, so the CPU faulted with:

user_prompt: explain what are the things that happen when I re-save platformio.ini and then VS Code seems to let PlatformIO extension do stuff silently in the background, it definitely recreates the `sdkconfig.defaults` file if I delete it
agent_reply: When you save `platformio.ini`, the PlatformIO VS Code extension notices the project config changed and does a background â€œproject refresh.â€ It is not just text editing. It can run PlatformIOâ€™s project/config processing quietly so IntelliSense, environments, dependencies, and generated build metadata stay current.
In your project, that likely means:
- It may run PlatformIOâ€™s Arduino-ESP32/ESP-IDF integration scripts.
- Those scripts generate or regenerate SDK config files used by the build system.
- If `sdkconfig.defaults` is missing, the integration can recreate it from the defaults/custom config source.
- VS Code C/C++ metadata may also refresh include paths, defines, and compile commands.

So the important bit: `sdkconfig.defaults` is not being recreated because VS Code â€œlikes that file.â€ It is being recreated because PlatformIO/ESP-IDF treats it as part of the build configuration pipeline.
That `custom_sdkconfig` block is probably the source of truth PlatformIO uses to synthesize/update `sdkconfig.defaults`. Saving `platformio.ini` causes that machinery to run again.
This also explains the IRAM weirdness: there are multiple config surfaces in play:
If the extension refreshes one layer but an old generated layer remains, you can see â€œwhy did IRAM change again?â€ behavior until the build fully regenerates or you delete the stale files.
My mental model for your repo:
- Treat `platformio.ini` `custom_sdkconfig` as the thing you actually edit.
- Treat `sdkconfig.defaults` as generated/derived unless you intentionally decide otherwise.
- Treat `.pio/packages/framework-arduinoespressif32/tools/sdk/esp32/sdkconfig` as unusual but currently intentionally tracked because you forced it in for this projectâ€™s IRAM behavior.

If you want to prove what happens, delete `sdkconfig.defaults`, save `platformio.ini`, then immediately check the file timestamp and contents. If it matches your `custom_sdkconfig` values, that is PlatformIO regenerating it from the INI.

user_prompt: so one clue is that if a build fails from the iram0 problem, and I do a full clean, and build again, I don't see the messages that tell me my custom_sdkconfig has been applied
if I re-save platformio.ini, let it process, full clean, and build, I get a gigantic blob of text saying which custom configs got applied
what does that mean and how do we take advantage of this fact?
agent_reply: That clue means the â€œcustom SDK config applicationâ€ step is **not reliably tied to `pio run` or full clean**. It is tied to a **PlatformIO project reconfiguration / metadata refresh** that VS Code triggers when `platformio.ini` changes.
- It does **not necessarily re-run the PlatformIO/extension config-materialization step**
- The giant blob is the moment where `custom_sdkconfig` is actually being materialized/applied
- If you do not see that blob, you may be building from stale SDK config state
How to take advantage: make that implicit VS Code side effect into an explicit build step.

Best path: add a small pre-build script that always verifies or regenerates the SDK config fragment from `platformio.ini`, then prints the same kind of â€œapplied configâ€ message. That way `pio run -e thefly_bringuptest` behaves like â€œsave platformio.ini, wait, clean, buildâ€ without needing the ritual.
Practical options, from least to most robust:
Add a pre-build script that checks generated `sdkconfig.defaults` contains your critical IRAM settings, and fails loudly if not.
Add a pre-build script that rewrites `sdkconfig.defaults` from `platformio.ini custom_sdkconfig` every time before build.

Make the script also delete only the env-local generated SDK config when the derived defaults change, for example `.pio/build/thefly_bringuptest/config/sdkconfig.h`, so the build cannot reuse stale config.

I would not rely on â€œresave `platformio.ini`â€ as the workflow. Itâ€™s a useful diagnostic: it tells us there is a missing explicit dependency in the build pipeline. The fix is to promote that hidden refresh into a deterministic script.

agent_reply: Yes, I think your read is right: this looks less like â€œbad `sdkconfig.defaults`â€ and more like **the generated build graph is sometimes pointing at a different framework/core/prebuilt library set**.
The current generated metadata says this build is using:
That is important because it is **not primarily using**:

So if you were tracking or modifying something under project-local `.pio/packages/...`, the build may simply not be using that path in the current CMake/PlatformIO mode. That would perfectly explain the â€œit feels like a pointer switchedâ€ behavior.
- Saving `platformio.ini` causes PlatformIO/VS Code to regenerate CMake/package metadata.
- That refresh can update `CMakeCache.txt`, `project_description.json`, package component paths, and dependency roots.
- A normal clean may delete objects but leave enough generated CMake/package metadata intact that it keeps using a stale or wrong package root.
- If a global package like `framework-arduinoespressif32-libs` is refreshed/reinstalled, your effective linked `.a` files can change without `sdkconfig.defaults` changing.
1. **Fingerprint the actual build inputs**, not the config file.
Track hashes/paths for the critical package roots and prebuilt libs:
2. **Add a pre-build guard** that reads `.pio/build/<env>/CMakeCache.txt` / `project_description.json` and fails loudly if the paths are not the known-good ones.
3. **Force reconfigure, not just clean**, when the pointer is stale.
The strongest move is to pin the package roots with `platform_packages` or a pre-build checker. Then the problem becomes visible immediately instead of showing up as a mysterious IRAM0 overflow.

user_prompt: fix the build error, then run the sterile rebuild
recall that we have a reoccuring problem where we build the project and suddenly iram0 overflows by like 2000+ bytes
we solve this by having a `custom_sdkconfig` in the `platformio.ini` with some settings that solve this problem (along with settings that enable hands-free-profile)
but still, during some builds, it seems like those settings become ignored and the default config is used, and iram0 overflows.
so I was suspecting that something is either being unzipped without us knowing and overwriting the custom built objects.

so I thought the problem was to just put good prebuilt files into packages that get downloaded and unzipped. I've hosted this on my own github, and pointed at them using `platformio.ini` under `platform_packages =`

`iram0_overflow_problem\good_working_build` is the build done with the `custom_sdkconfig`, you can see the `platformio.ini` and the `custom_sdkconfig` there. There is also a `firmware.map` file to analyze.

`iram0_overflow_problem\bad_build_with_default_config` is the build artifacts from the default config build, you can see `iram0_overflow_problem\bad_build_with_default_config\build_error.txt` that says `region `iram0_0_seg' overflowed by 308 bytes`, plus a missing function `esp_hf_client_outgoing_data_ready`. As before, the `firmware.map` file is available to analyze, and also `platformio.ini`

`iram0_overflow_problem\bad_build_with_custom_framework_files` are the built files from when the custom framework zip files are used. The zip file contents are available at `iram0_overflow_problem\custom-framework-arduinoespressif32` and `iram0_overflow_problem\custom-framework-arduinoespressif32-libs`. I don't know if I made these correctly. The `firmware.map` file is available, as well as the `platformio.ini` and `build_error.txt` as before.
analyze all of these, especially figure out where the iram0 differences are, see how I can fix this, hopefully I swap out some bad files with good files, zip everything up, upload, and it all works.
agent_reply: Yep, the culprit is pretty clear now.
| good custom sdkconfig | `0x1dd73` / 122,227 B | fits, about 7.8 KB headroom |
| bad default config | `0x1fd2f` / 130,351 B | overflows by 308 B |
| bad custom framework zips | `0x1ff43` / 130,883 B | overflows by 840 B |

The big IRAM delta is not mainly Bluetooth. It is `libc.a` being pulled into IRAM. In the bad/custom-package map, about 8.9 KB extra comes from routines like `mktime`, `tzset_r`, `findfp`, `fvwrite`, `fflush`, `strtol`, etc. That matches the `CONFIG_SPIRAM_CACHE_LIB*IN_IRAM` settings being effectively â€œonâ€ in the linker script.

The custom package fixes the missing HFP symbol: `esp_hf_client_outgoing_data_ready` is present in the custom-package map, while the default build only has the unresolved reference from `BluetoothManager.cpp`.
The remaining failure is this file:
It is still default/upstream and explicitly places lots of `libc.a:*` objects into `.iram0.text`. The good buildâ€™s generated linker script does not:
For the custom zip approach, replace these files in both package trees, then publish as a new release tag like `r2`:
2. Copy good sdkconfig header for consistency:
- to both `esp32/dio_qspi/include/sdkconfig.h` and `esp32/qio_qspi/include/sdkconfig.h` in the libs package
- also update the nested copy under `custom-framework-arduinoespressif32/tools/esp32-arduino-libs/...`
3. Do not package `.piopm` files if you can avoid it. Let PlatformIO regenerate them.
4. Use a new URL/tag in `platformio.ini`; do not overwrite the same `r1` asset and expect PlatformIO to notice reliably.

Also, current [platformio.ini](D:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/platformio.ini:8) has the custom package URLs but the `custom_sdkconfig` block was removed. That is why the build no longer regenerates the good `sections.ld`. Either restore the `custom_sdkconfig` block as a safety net, or make the custom package fully self-contained by fixing `sections.ld` as above. My preference: do both until this is boringly stable.

agent_reply: Yep, that explains the stack traces. Pairing completion was letting the phone immediately kick off HFP service setup, which is the fragile Bluedroid path.
I changed it so boot/pairing use Bluetooth GAP only, and HFP is initialized lazily when you actually connect to a saved host:
- Changed `setup()` in [main.cpp](D:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/src/main.cpp) to use GAP-only init, while still storing audio callbacks.
- Changed `startPairing()` in [BluetoothManager.cpp](D:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/BluetoothManager/BluetoothManager.cpp) to avoid HFP during pairing, and deinit HFP before pairing if it was already up.
- Left the internal HFP debug flags disabled in [platformio.ini](D:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/platformio.ini), since theyâ€™re still too close to the WDT path for comfort.
Verified with `pio run -e thefly_sec0`, build passes.

agent_reply: That trace is still the same Bluedroid HFP SLC path, now specifically dying while sending `AT+BAC` for codec negotiation.

I added a targeted workaround: [BluetoothManager.cpp](D:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/BluetoothManager/BluetoothManager.cpp) now wraps Bluedroidâ€™s internal `bta_hf_client_send_at_bac()` and skips straight to the next SLC step (`AT+CIND`). [platformio.ini](D:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/platformio.ini) adds the linker wrap flag. This should avoid the crashing `AT+BAC` path and force the connection toward default/CVSD audio instead of mSBC/wideband.
I also kept the previous GAP-only pairing changes, so pairing no longer starts HFP immediately.

Verified with `pio run -e thefly_sec0`, build passes. The tradeoff is that HFP audio may be narrowband only for now, but that is a much better place to debug from than â€œphone click detonates inside Bluedroid.â€

`M5GFX` is M5Stack’s wrapper around LovyanGFX. In our app, [src/main.cpp](C:/GitHubRepos/The-Fly/src/main.cpp:88) sets:
and [src/all_init.cpp](C:/GitHubRepos/The-Fly/src/all_init.cpp:107) initializes it through `M5.begin(cfg)`, then sets brightness/color depth and clears the screen at lines 115-117.

So on our Core2 LCD path, drawing is basically **immediate-mode streaming over SPI into the LCD controller’s GRAM**. Calls like `fillRect`, `drawString`, and `pushImage` send pixels directly. There is no app-side full framebuffer that needs `.display()` to “present.”

The catch was that sterile rebuild only works if the custom-sdkconfig path is actually usable. I added the missing support around that:

- [tools/patch_pioarduino_custom_sdkconfig.py](/d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/tools/patch_pioarduino_custom_sdkconfig.py) patches the pioarduino builder edge cases for inherited `custom_sdkconfig` and generated Arduino component names.
- [CMakeLists.txt](/d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/CMakeLists.txt:3) now detects the generated `framework-arduinoespressif32...` component name instead of hardcoding one.
- [platformio.ini](/d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/platformio.ini:43) wires in the patch script and keeps your custom sdkconfig changes active.
Only remaining build output is existing deprecation warnings; the IRAM overflow is gone.
